title: Adaptive Media Delivery (AMD)
subtitle: ABR video delivery at global scale

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: AMD is like a waiter who reads the room. If the diner (viewer) is on fast Wi-Fi, serve the steak (4K). If they're on a slow connection, serve the soup (480p). The kitchen (origin) prepares all versions; AMD picks the right one for each guest.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Adaptive Media Delivery is Akamai's CDN product purpose-built for video. It handles:

- HLS and DASH manifest delivery and segment caching
- ABR (adaptive bitrate) — the player requests segments at the quality matching current bandwidth
- Token authentication to protect premium streams
- Tiered distribution — shield origins from thundering-herd cache misses
- Mid-tier caching and segment prefetching for live events

AMD is configured in Property Manager, just like Ion, but with video-specific behaviors: manifest manipulation, segment duration hints, live-to-VOD transition rules.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Every major live sports broadcast on Akamai runs through AMD. The typical architecture: encoder (Ateme/Bitmovin) → origin (Unified Streaming or MSL) → AMD (CDN delivery) → player. AMD handles millions of concurrent viewers per event, with mid-tier caching shielding the origin from segment storms.
"AMD = Akamai's video CDN. HLS/DASH delivery, ABR, token auth, tiered caching — configured in Property Manager like any other property."

quiz.question: What makes AMD different from Ion (Akamai's standard web CDN)?

options.explain: AMD has video-specific optimizations: manifest manipulation, segment caching, ABR awareness, live event handling.

options.text: AMD is optimized for HLS/DASH video delivery with manifest manipulation, segment caching, and live event support
options.explain: Ion is for web pages and APIs. AMD understands video manifests, segments, and live-vs-VOD transitions.
Pricing depends on traffic volume. AMD's value is in video-specific features.

options.text: AMD only works with Akamai encoders
options.explain: AMD works with any origin that produces HLS/DASH — Akamai or third-party.

quiz.question: During a live sports event, millions of viewers request the same segment simultaneously. What AMD feature prevents origin overload?

options.explain: You don't want to rate-limit legitimate viewers during a live event.

options.text: Tiered distribution with mid-tier caching — shields the origin behind cache layers
options.explain: Mid-tier caches absorb the thundering herd. Only one request per segment reaches origin; the rest are served from cache.
You want to serve all quality levels. Caching handles the load.
That defeats the purpose of having your own delivery infrastructure.
